body:
`GetterValidator` has an unused import (`ElementKind`). In Java this is a compilation error and will break the build; remove the unused import (or use it if intended).
```suggestion

```

Linked Issue #20 states `@Getter` should only be allowed on **private** fields, but the validator currently allows any visibility. If private-only is still required, add a validation rule that emits an ERROR when the field is not `private` (and update tests/docs accordingly). If not required, consider updating the issue/specs to match the intended API.

`checkCovariantReturnTypes` relies on `superField.getAnnotation(rawit.Getter.class)` to detect `@Getter` on superclass fields. Because `@Getter` is `RetentionPolicy.SOURCE`, this annotation is not present in already-compiled superclass `.class` files, so subclasses compiled in a later/other module wonΓÇÖt detect the base `@Getter` and the covariant-override rules canΓÇÖt be applied correctly. To make this robust across compilation boundaries, consider marking injected getter methods in bytecode (e.g., a CLASS-retained marker annotation or ACC_SYNTHETIC + known attribute) and detect based on the inherited method instead of the (non-existent) field annotation.

`checkInheritedMethodCollisions` uses `isGetterAnnotatedField(...)`, which checks `field.getAnnotation(rawit.Getter.class)` on superclass fields to decide whether an inherited zero-arg method was generated by `@Getter`. Since `@Getter` has SOURCE retention, that annotation will be absent on classpath-only superclasses, causing generated getters to be misclassified as ΓÇ£manualΓÇ¥ and triggering false collision errors in subclasses. Consider switching to a bytecode-level marker on the generated getter method (or CLASS retention on a marker annotation) so inherited-method classification works when the superclass is already compiled.

The test treats `@Getter` on anonymous-class fields as ΓÇ£silently ignoredΓÇ¥, but the requirements doc (11.1) and `GetterValidator` implementation both define this as a compile-time ERROR (`@Getter is not supported inside anonymous classes`). This inconsistency means requirement 11.1 isnΓÇÖt actually being asserted. Align the expected behavior (either assert an error here, or update the requirements/docs/validator if silent ignore is the intended behavior).

This property test claims anonymous-class `@Getter` fields are ΓÇ£not discoveredΓÇ¥ and expects no diagnostics, but that conflicts with requirement 11.1 and `GetterValidator` (which emits an ERROR for anonymous-class fields). Because of the mismatch, the property isnΓÇÖt validating the stated requirement; update the property to assert the error, or update the requirements/docs/validator if silent ignore is actually desired.

The debug log prefix/message strings used in the new `@Getter` branch still use `"[invoker.debug]"`. That makes diagnostics harder to interpret when debugging getter injection vs invoker/constructor processing. Consider using a more generic prefix (e.g. `[rawit.debug]`) or a getter-specific one while still honoring the same `invoker.debug` flag (or rename the option if appropriate).
I have decided that allowing it on any visibility modifiers makes more sense. Objection overruled.

`checkCovariantReturnTypes` uses `typeUtils.erasure(...)` on both types before `isSubtype`, which will incorrectly treat invariant generic types as compatible (e.g., `List<Integer>` would be accepted as a subtype of `List<Number>` after erasure). This can allow invalid overrides that Java would reject, and can also cause incorrect error behavior for parameterized fields. Consider using `typeUtils.isSubtype(derivedType, baseType)` (without erasure) for declared/parameterized types, and only erasing where itΓÇÖs actually required (e.g., type variables).
```suggestion
                        && !typeUtils.isSubtype(derivedType, baseType)) {
```

`isGetterAnnotatedField(...)` treats a superclass method as ΓÇ£@Getter-generatedΓÇ¥ whenever a field *would* compute to the same getter name and a zero-arg method with that name exists, even if the field is not actually annotated. This misclassifies manual getters and can suppress required inherited-collision errors (Req 12.1) and also trigger covariant checks when only one side is annotated. To make this reliable across compilations with SOURCE retention, consider marking injected getters (e.g., `ACC_SYNTHETIC` and/or a CLASS-retained marker annotation) and checking for that marker on the method instead of inferring from `field + method name` alone.

`ExecutableElement#getAnnotation(GeneratedGetter.class)` follows reflection semantics and typically only returns non-null for RUNTIME-retained annotations. Since `@GeneratedGetter` is `RetentionPolicy.CLASS`, this cross-compilation detection will likely never detect injected getters on already-compiled superclasses, causing false inherited-collision errors and skipping covariant override checks. Use `getAnnotationMirrors()` (match by FQN) to detect `@GeneratedGetter`, or change the marker retention to RUNTIME if thatΓÇÖs acceptable.

Same issue as above: `getAnnotation(GeneratedGetter.class)` is unlikely to work for a CLASS-retained marker. Prefer scanning `method.getAnnotationMirrors()` for `rawit.processors.inject.GeneratedGetter` to make cross-compilation detection reliable.
